figures.caption: Fig. 4: Real-world flying arena for autonomous quadcopter setup. A 6 × 6 meters room comprises a serie of OptiTrack motion capture cameras for reading the position and rotational information through the reflection of infrared marker on a Crazyflie drone.

Fig. 3: The simulated OGN scene comprises an outdoor landscape with a flight area of 2.3×2.3×2 meters, a Crazyflie quadcopter, and a red barrel as the target object. The target can appear in 24 different locations on three levels, each level comprises 8 grids at 0.5m height, as depicted above. The second and third levels are located at 1.025 and 1.55 meters height, respectively. At each episode, the agent starts from the same initial position at (x = 0, y = 0, z = 1).
Fig. 14: Real-world trajectories towards the target located at (−0.95,−0.95, 1.55) for TD3-AmelPredSto. Three different runs are depicted in (a), (b), and (c).
Fig. 12: Real-world trajectories towards the target located at (0.95,−0.95, 0.5) for TD3-AmelPredSto. Three different runs are depicted in (a), (b), and (c).
Fig. 13: Real-world trajectories towards the target located at (−0.95, 0.0, 1.02) for TD3-AmelPredSto. Three different runs are depicted in (a), (b), and (c).

Fig. 1: The overall architecture of Chemamuy ANS for solving control, perception, and planning tasks with a quadrotor. On the UAV side, a PID-based control system deals with the UAV dynamics while continuously transmitting its sensor readings. On the Station side, the sensor data is processed by an SRLbased perception method, creating a latent representation z further processed by RL. The RL approach sends the desired velocities to displace the UAV towards the target.
Fig. 8: Interquartile mean (IQM) reward curve comparison of vanilla algorithms and prediction-based methods, with five different seeds value.
TABLE I: Computational cost comparison using sensor-based target-perception in observation.
Fig. 2: AmelPredDet architecture with a deterministic encoder function.

Fig. 11: Success path length (SPL) metric comparison of vanilla algorithms and prediction-based methods. The colored lines depicts the mean value of five agents during evaluation, with the shaded area as the standard deviation.
Fig. 9: Performance profile comparison of vanilla algorithms and prediction-based methods.

Fig. 10: Distance to success (DTS) metric comparison of vanilla algorithms and prediction-based methods. The colored lines depicts the mean value of five agents during evaluation, with the shaded area as the standard deviation.
